operating_state: Total revenues increased 60% to $35.69 billion for the six months driven by automotive sales growth from 179,242 additional unit deliveries and higher average selling prices from increased Model Y proportion. Production ramping at Shanghai and Fremont facilities, combined with new production at Berlin-Brandenburg and Texas Gigafactories, supported delivery growth despite global semiconductor shortages and COVID-19 disruptions requiring alternative parts and software programming. Energy generation and storage revenue grew 14% primarily from increased Powerwall deployments, partially offset by lower Megapack volumes.
cost_structure: Total automotive gross margin increased from 27.5% to 30.6% for the six months, driven by revenue growth and regulatory credits increases, partially offset by idle capacity charges from Shanghai shutdowns and Texas ramp-up plus rising raw material, commodity, logistics and expedite costs. Raw material cost inflation, particularly lithium, nickel, and cobalt for battery cells, pressures margins as suppliers may be unable to meet volume needs or cost targets. Energy generation and storage gross margin improved from -6.3% to 1.7% primarily from higher-margin Powerwall deployments.
material_events: Recorded $170 million impairment loss on bitcoin holdings and realized $64 million gains from digital asset conversions during the six months. Began delivering Model Y vehicles with Tesla-made 4680 cells and structural battery packs at Texas Gigafactory while commencing production at Berlin-Brandenburg. Recorded expenses related to recent employee terminations during the second quarter.
macro_exposures: COVID-19 spikes in Shanghai resulted in temporary Gigafactory shutdowns and supply chain disruptions during the first and second quarters. Industry-wide semiconductor and component shortages continue requiring additional workaround manufacturing solutions and expedited delivery costs. Heightened exposure to geopolitical instability from Russia-Ukraine conflict including potential embargoes, airspace restrictions, and retaliatory actions, plus currency exposure to Chinese yuan, euro, and pound sterling affects revenue translation and cost structure.
forward_outlook: Management expects capital expenditures of $6.00 to $8.00 billion in 2022 and each of the next two fiscal years to support global projects focused on increasing vehicle production capacity, improving battery technologies and FSD capabilities, increasing vehicle affordability and expanding global infrastructure. Continuing to ramp energy storage production, improve Solar Roof installation efficiency, and increase market penetration while expecting business to remain self-funding as long as macroeconomic factors support current sales trends.
uncertainty_profile: Production ramps at new facilities subject to uncertainties from new product and manufacturing technologies, concurrent international projects, and potential industry-wide component constraints. Digital assets subject to impairment charges for fair value decreases below carrying values, with no upward revisions until sale. Regulatory challenges persist regarding direct-to-consumer vehicle sales models with ongoing litigation and legislative efforts, while autonomous driving feature development faces evolving regulatory frameworks that may restrict functionality or delay deployment.
